resources.string: Shows your device\'s privacy score at a glance
Device should have a screen lock enabled (PIN, pattern, password, or biometric)
Enable a screen lock in Settings > Security > Screen lock to protect your device from unauthorized access.
Device storage should be encrypted to protect your data if the device is lost or stolen
Enable device encryption in Settings > Security > Encryption (typically enabled by default on modern devices).
Biometric authentication should be enabled for added security
Set up fingerprint or face unlock in Settings > Security > Biometrics for convenient and secure authentication.
USB debugging should be disabled for security
Disable USB debugging in Settings > Developer options to prevent unauthorized access via USB connection.
Developer options should be disabled on personal devices
Disable developer options in Settings > System > Developer options to reduce security risks.
Apps with notification listener permission can read ALL notifications including 2FA codes and messages
Review and revoke notification access for non-essential apps in Settings > Apps > Special app access > Notification access.
Apps with accessibility services can control your entire device and potentially log your keystrokes
Disable accessibility services for apps that don\'t legitimately need them in Settings > Accessibility.
Apps with device admin privileges have elevated control over your device
Review and remove device admin access for non-essential apps in Settings > Security > Device admin apps.
Apps with background location permission can track your precise location at all times, even when not in use
Review and restrict background location permissions in Settings > Location > App permissions. Only grant background location to apps that genuinely need it.
Your device\'s security patch date shows when it last received fixes for known vulnerabilities. Older patches leave your device exposed to security risks that have since been fixed.
Check for system updates in Settings > System > System update. If no updates are available, your device may be end-of-life and no longer supported by the manufacturer.
VPN should be active to protect network traffic
Enable a VPN connection to encrypt your internet traffic and protect your privacy online.
Private DNS encrypts your web lookups so your internet provider can\'t see which sites you visit
Configure Private DNS in Settings > Network & internet > Private DNS. Recommended: dns.quad9.net (privacy-focused, blocks malware) or dns.adguard-dns.com (blocks ads and trackers).
Advertising ID enables cross-app tracking by advertisers
Disable or reset your Advertising ID in Settings > Privacy > Ads.
Not applicable — no Google Play Services
Your phone silently broadcasts its identity to nearby networks even when Wi-Fi is off. Shops, malls, and airports already use this to track how long you stay and how often you visit.
Disable Wi-Fi and Bluetooth scanning in Settings > Location > Wi-Fi and Bluetooth scanning to prevent location tracking.
Google\'s Find My Device / Find Hub tracks device location — a security feature with a privacy cost. On Android 14+ the enabled state cannot be programmatically detected.
Disable in Settings > Security > Find My Device (or Find Hub on Android 14+) if privacy is a priority over theft protection.
Google Play Services has deep access to your device and enables extensive tracking and data collection across all apps
Consider using GrapheneOS to run Google Play Services with limited access, significantly reducing its reach on your device.
Switch to a privacy-respecting browser like Brave, Firefox, or DuckDuckGo Browser. Available on Google Play Store.
Switch to FUTO Keyboard (Play Store) for best privacy + features with offline voice typing, or AnySoftKeyboard (F-Droid & Play Store) for a mature, highly customizable option.
Privacy-invasive messaging app detected as default
Switch to Fossify Messages - available on F-Droid and Play Store. Open source, privacy-focused, no ads.
Privacy-invasive email app detected as default
Switch to a privacy-respecting email client like K-9 Mail, FairEmail, or ProtonMail. Available on Google Play Store.
Switch to a privacy-respecting launcher like Lawnchair, KISS Launcher, or Neo Launcher. Available on Google Play Store.
Google Chrome app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Chrome and switching to Brave, Firefox, or DuckDuckGo Browser.
Gmail app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Gmail and switching to K-9 Mail, FairEmail, or ProtonMail.
Google Maps app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Google Maps and switching to OsmAnd or Organic Maps.
Google Photos app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Google Photos and switching to Simple Gallery or Aves Libre.
Google Drive app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Google Drive and switching to Nextcloud or Cryptomator.
YouTube app is installed on your device
Consider using NewPipe or LibreTube as privacy-friendly YouTube alternatives.
Google Calendar app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Google Calendar and switching to Simple Calendar or an offline calendar app.
Google Keep app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Google Keep and switching to Simple Notes or Standard Notes.
Google Camera app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Google Camera and switching to Open Camera or Simple Camera.
Google Docs app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Google Docs and switching to Collabora Office or an offline editor.
Facebook app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling the Facebook app and using the mobile website if needed.
Instagram app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling the Instagram app and using the mobile website if needed, or switching to Pixelfed.
WhatsApp is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling WhatsApp and switching to Signal, Session, or SimpleX Chat.
Facebook Messenger app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Messenger and switching to Signal or another privacy-focused messaging app.
Microsoft Edge app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Edge and switching to Brave, Firefox, or DuckDuckGo Browser.
Microsoft Outlook app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Outlook and switching to K-9 Mail, FairEmail, or ProtonMail.
Microsoft OneDrive app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling OneDrive and switching to Nextcloud or Cryptomator.
Amazon Shopping app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling and using the mobile website for better privacy.
Amazon Prime Video app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling Prime Video and using the mobile website instead. Streaming apps collect viewing habits and device data.
AI apps process conversations on cloud servers — be mindful of what you share
Avoid sharing passwords, financial details, or personal information in AI conversations. Review your chat history and data settings in the app.
TikTok app is installed on your device
Consider uninstalling TikTok due to extensive data collection practices.
Twitter/X app is installed on your device
Consider using the mobile website instead of the app for better privacy.
Reddit app is installed on your device
Consider using privacy-focused Reddit clients like Infinity or Slide.
Voice assistants can collect and process your conversations and search history
Review your assistant settings in Settings > Apps and disable Google Assistant or Alexa if you don\'t use them.
Most apps don\'t need 24/7 location tracking. Review which apps have "Always" access.
Many apps over-request camera and microphone permissions. Review which apps can access them.
Unused apps still have permissions and potential vulnerabilities. Find and remove apps you haven\'t used.
Verify your Advertising ID is deleted to prevent cross-app tracking by advertisers.

Apps with "Always" location access can track your movements 24/7, even when you\'re not using the app. Most apps only need location "While using the app" or not at all. Continuous tracking drains battery, creates detailed movement profiles, and poses significant privacy risks if the app is compromised or shares data with third parties.

Camera and microphone permissions allow apps to potentially record you at any time. Many apps request these permissions unnecessarily or "just in case" you might use a feature. A compromised app with these permissions could spy on you. Regular audits help ensure only trusted apps can access these sensitive sensors.

Unused apps are a hidden privacy and security risk. They still have all their permissions, continue receiving data access, may have unpatched vulnerabilities, and increase your attack surface. Apps you installed months or years ago might have been updated with new tracking features or sold to different companies. Regular cleanups reduce these risks.

Your Advertising ID is a unique identifier that lets advertisers link your activity across different apps and services to build a profile of your habits and interests. Deleting it resets this profile and prevents further cross-app behavioral tracking until a new ID is created.
Look for apps with "Allowed all the time" or "Always" access
For each app, tap it and change to "Allow only while using the app" or "Don\'t allow"
Tap "Camera" to see all apps with camera access
Review each app — ask yourself: "Does this app really need camera access?"
Revoke camera access for apps that don\'t need it
Go back and repeat for "Microphone" permissions
Navigate to Apps or Application Manager
Look for a sort option and select "Last used" or "Unused apps"
Scroll through the list and identify apps you haven\'t opened in months
For each unused app, ask: "Will I actually use this again?"
Uninstall apps you don\'t need — you can always reinstall later if needed
Maps and navigation apps: These may legitimately need "Always" for features like traffic alerts, but consider if you really use those features
Weather apps: Usually don\'t need "Always" access — they work fine with "While using the app"
Social media apps: Almost never need "Always" location access
Shopping or delivery apps: Only need location when actively using them
Unknown or rarely-used apps: If you don\'t recognize an app with always-on access, revoke it immediately
Games that don\'t use voice or AR features
Shopping apps with no scanning features
Any app you don\'t recognize or haven\'t used recently
Verify that your Advertising ID has been deleted in Settings → Privacy → Ads.
Old games you no longer play
Abandoned trial apps or free trials
Apps for events or trips that already happened
Duplicate apps (multiple weather apps, calculators, etc.)
Apps from companies you no longer trust
System apps (be careful not to remove critical system components)
Banking or financial apps (even if used infrequently)
Travel apps if you travel regularly
Tip: If you\'re unsure whether you\'ll need an app again, uninstall it anyway. Most apps keep your account data server-side, so you can reinstall and log back in if needed.
Non-system apps can read ALL your notifications

Apps with notification listener access can read ALL your notifications, including 2FA codes and private messages.

Steps:
1. Open Settings app
2. Go to 'Apps' → 'Special app access'
3. Tap 'Notification access'
4. Review the list and disable access for non-essential apps

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!
Non-essential apps can control your entire device

Apps with accessibility service access can control your entire device and potentially log keystrokes.

Steps:
1. Open Settings app
2. Go to 'Accessibility'
3. Review 'Downloaded apps' or 'Installed services'
4. Disable any services you don't actively need

Note: Keep accessibility services that you genuinely use (password managers, screen readers).

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!
Non-system apps have elevated device control

Apps with device administrator privileges have elevated control over your device.

Steps:
1. Open Settings app
2. Go to 'Security' → 'Device admin apps'
3. Review the list of device administrators
4. Deactivate any that you don't recognize or need

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!
Your phone is visible to nearby tracking systems even with Wi-Fi off

Even with Wi-Fi turned off, your phone regularly broadcasts a signal that nearby receivers can pick up. Shopping malls, airports, and retailers already use this commercially to track visitor movements. Disabling it makes your phone invisible to these systems with no noticeable impact on daily use.

Steps:
1. Open Settings app
2. Go to 'Location'
3. Tap 'Location services' or 'Wi-Fi and Bluetooth scanning'
4. Disable 'Wi-Fi scanning'

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!

This prevents apps from building an advertising profile and tracking you across different apps.

Steps:
1. Open Settings app
2. Scroll to 'Privacy' or 'Google'
3. Tap 'Ads'
4. Tap 'Delete Advertising ID' or 'Opt out of Ads Personalization'
5. Confirm

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!
Blocks tracking and malicious domains at network level

Private DNS blocks tracking and malicious domains at the network level for all apps.

Recommended providers:
• Quad9 (dns.quad9.net)
 Privacy-focused, blocks malware, no logging

• AdGuard DNS (dns.adguard-dns.com)
 Blocks ads and trackers

Steps:
1. Open Settings app
2. Go to 'Network & Internet'
3. Tap 'Private DNS'
4. Select 'Private DNS provider hostname'
5. Enter: dns.quad9.net
6. Tap 'Save'

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!
Reduces your exposure to advanced settings that can weaken security

Developer options expose advanced settings that can reduce device security if misconfigured.

Steps:
1. Open Settings app
2. Go to 'System' → 'Developer options'
3. Toggle off 'Developer options' at the top
4. Confirm

Note: If you need developer options for specific tasks, re-enable only when needed.

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!

Privacy-focused browsers block trackers and don't sync your browsing history to cloud servers.

Recommended alternatives:
• Brave (Play Store) - Blocks trackers by default
• Firefox (F-Droid and Play Store) - Privacy-focused, customizable

Or explore more options:
→ Privacy Guides: privacyguides.org/browsers

Steps:
1. Open F-Droid or Play Store
2. Search 'Brave' or 'Firefox'
3. Install browser
4. Open the new browser
5. When prompted, set as default browser
 (Or: Settings → Apps → Default apps → Browser)

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!

Your current keyboard may send your typing data to cloud servers for processing.

Recommended alternatives:
• FUTO Keyboard (Play Store)
 Best privacy + features, offline voice typing
• AnySoftKeyboard (F-Droid & Play Store)
 Mature, highly customizable

Or explore more options:
→ F-Droid: Browse privacy keyboards
→ Privacy Guides: privacyguides.org/android

Steps:
1. Open F-Droid or Play Store
2. Search 'privacy keyboard'
3. Install keyboard
4. Open Settings → System → Languages & Input
5. Tap 'On-screen keyboard' or 'Virtual keyboard'
6. Select your new keyboard as default

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!

Your current SMS app may collect your messaging data and contacts.

Recommended alternatives:
• Fossify Messages (F-Droid & Play Store)
 Open source, privacy-focused, no ads
• QKSMS (F-Droid)
 Lightweight and private

Steps:
1. Install a privacy-focused messaging app
2. Open Settings → Apps → Default apps
3. Tap 'SMS app'
4. Select your new messaging app

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!

Your current email app may scan your emails for advertising and data collection.

Recommended alternatives:
• K-9 Mail (F-Droid & Play Store) - Open source
• FairEmail (F-Droid & Play Store) - Privacy-focused
• ProtonMail (Play Store) - Encrypted email

Steps:
1. Install a privacy-focused email app
2. Open Settings → Apps → Default apps
3. Tap 'Email app' (if available)
4. Select your new email app

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!

Your current launcher may collect usage patterns, app habits, and behavioral data.

Recommended alternatives:
• Lawnchair (F-Droid & Play Store) - Modern and customizable
• KISS Launcher (F-Droid & Play Store) - Lightweight
• Neo Launcher (F-Droid) - Feature-rich

Steps:
1. Install a privacy-focused launcher
2. Open Settings → Apps → Default apps
3. Tap 'Home app'
4. Select your new launcher

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!
Remove a privacy-invasive app from your device

You can improve your privacy by uninstalling or disabling this app.

Steps:
1. Tap 'Open App Settings' below
2. Tap 'Uninstall' or 'Disable'
3. Confirm

If the app came pre-installed, you may only be able to disable it rather than fully uninstall it.

After completing this, rescan to see your improved score!
USB Debugging allows connected computers to access your device data. It\'s essential for development but best left off when you\'re not actively using it.
Developer Options are powerful tools for debugging, but they expose settings that can weaken security. Toggle it off when you don\'t need it — you can always re-enable it.
Without a VPN, your internet provider sees every domain you connect to. Some providers sell this data to advertisers. A VPN keeps your browsing between you and the VPN provider.
Your Advertising ID lets ad networks build a profile across every app you use. Deleting it doesn\'t break any apps — they just lose the ability to connect your activity.
Without Private DNS, your DNS lookups are sent unencrypted to your provider. Enabling it takes 30 seconds and blocks known trackers across all your apps automatically.
Google Maps is excellent, but it logs every search, route, and visit to your Google account. If you mainly need offline navigation, Organic Maps offers that without any tracking.
Apps with notification access can read all your notifications — messages, banking alerts, verification codes. Worth reviewing whether each app truly needs this level of access.
Adding biometric authentication makes it faster to unlock your device, which means you\'re more likely to keep a strong PIN. Convenience and security working together.

Even with Wi-Fi off, your phone broadcasts signals that shops and malls use to track how long you visit and how often you return. Disabling background scanning makes you invisible to these systems with no impact on daily use.
Find My Device is useful if your phone is lost, but it requires Google to know your device\'s location. If you\'re on GrapheneOS without Play Services, this isn\'t functional anyway.
Chrome syncs browsing data with your Google account by default. Firefox or Brave offer similar speed with better default privacy settings — and your bookmarks can be imported.
Gboard sends typing data to Google to improve predictions. Privacy-focused keyboards like HeliBoard or FlorisBoard work offline with no data collection.
Your screen lock is your first defense. A 6-digit PIN takes around 11 hours to brute force, while a 4-digit PIN takes just 7 minutes. Length matters more than complexity.
With encryption enabled, your phone\'s data is unreadable without your PIN when powered off — even with physical access to the storage chip.

Your biometric data stays in your device\'s secure enclave. It never leaves the chip, isn\'t sent to any server, and can\'t be extracted even by the OS itself. For higher-risk situations — such as border crossings or legal proceedings — a strong PIN offers additional protection, as it cannot be physically compelled.
Your Private DNS works across every app — even ones that try to use their own tracking domains. It\'s one of the most effective single settings on your device.
Your VPN encrypts all traffic leaving your device. On public WiFi, others on the network only see encrypted data going to one server — nothing useful.
Without an Advertising ID, apps can still show ads, but they can\'t easily build a cross-app profile of your interests and behavior. The ads just become less targeted.
Running without Google apps means significantly less background data collection. Most Google services have capable alternatives — it\'s about finding what works for you.
Photos contain hidden metadata — GPS coordinates, timestamp, device model. Before sharing sensitive photos, strip this data using apps like Scrambled Exif. It takes one tap.
Public WiFi is easy to impersonate. Anyone can create a network called \'Airport_Free_WiFi\'. A VPN makes this a non-issue since all your traffic is encrypted regardless.
Browser fingerprinting identifies you by combining your screen size, fonts, timezone, and GPU into a unique signature. This works even without cookies. Firefox has the best built-in protections.
SMS-based two-factor authentication is better than nothing, but vulnerable to SIM swap attacks. App-based authenticators like Aegis or Google Authenticator are significantly safer.
Free VPNs need revenue — most sell your browsing data or inject ads. Privacy-respecting VPNs like Mullvad or Proton VPN cost a few euros per month and have audited no-log policies.
Your phone broadcasts WiFi probe requests — searching for known networks by name. This can reveal places you\'ve been. Disabling auto-join for old networks reduces this leakage.
Clipboard data is accessible to any foreground app on older Android versions. Use your password manager\'s autofill instead of copying passwords — it\'s both safer and faster.
Your device name is visible to anyone nearby scanning Bluetooth or WiFi Direct. Setting it to something generic like \'Phone\' prevents it from identifying you personally.
Deleting an app removes it from your device but not from the company\'s servers. Use \'Delete Account\' in the app first, then uninstall. GDPR gives EU residents the legal right to request deletion.
Email is not private by default — it\'s sent in plain text between servers. For sensitive communication, use Signal or encrypted email like Proton Mail. Regular email is fine for newsletters.
Data brokers compile profiles from public records, purchases, and app data, then sell them. Services like Mine or yourdigitalrights.org help you discover and request deletion of your data.
Incognito mode only hides browsing from other people using your device. Your ISP, employer, and the websites themselves still see everything. A VPN addresses the ISP part.
App permissions on Android are revocable at any time. Granting camera access to scan a QR code doesn\'t mean the app needs it forever — review and revoke after one-time use.

Search engines log every query tied to your account or IP. DuckDuckGo and Startpage provide Google-quality results without building a search profile. You can always fall back to Google for specific queries.
Email trackers — invisible pixels embedded in marketing emails — report when and where you opened them. Disabling \'auto-load images\' in your email app blocks most of these.
Cloud backups are convenient but mean your data exists on someone else\'s server. If you use cloud backup, ensure it\'s encrypted. GrapheneOS\'s Seedvault offers encrypted local backups.
Location data is among the most sensitive information your phone generates. Even anonymized location datasets have been de-anonymized — the path between your home and work is unique to you.
App updates aren\'t just about new features — they patch security vulnerabilities. Keeping apps updated is one of the simplest and most effective security practices.
QR codes can link to malicious websites just like any URL. Before scanning unknown QR codes in public places, consider that someone may have placed a sticker over the original.
Your phone\'s sensors — accelerometer, gyroscope, barometer — can be accessed by any app without special permission. Research has shown these can be used for crude keystroke detection.
Using email aliases (like SimpleLogin or addy.io) means each service gets a unique address. If one gets breached or sold, you disable that alias instead of changing your actual email everywhere.
Android\'s auto-revoke automatically removes permissions from apps you haven\'t used in months. This is on by default since Android 11 — one of the better automatic privacy features.
Paying with a card creates a permanent record of where you were and what you bought. Cash or privacy-focused payment methods break this tracking chain for sensitive purchases.
Social media apps often request contacts access during setup. This uploads your entire address book — including phone numbers of people who never consented to sharing their data.
Smart home devices process some audio in the cloud. If you use them, review and regularly delete stored voice recordings in the manufacturer\'s app.

Your router\'s DNS settings affect every device on your network. Setting your router\'s DNS to a privacy-respecting provider (Quad9, NextDNS) protects all devices at once — including smart TVs and IoT.
Trusting an app removes its privacy score penalty. Apps from major data brokers cannot be trusted.

Some deductions reflect deliberate trade-offs — keeping USB Debugging on for development, or allowing a trusted app notification access. These are your informed choices, not privacy gaps.\n\nA perfect 100 is theoretically possible on a fully de-Googled device with no privacy trade-offs.

Privamatic gives your Android device a privacy score — a clear, actionable number that shows exactly how exposed you are and what to fix.

All analysis runs entirely on your device. No internet connection, no telemetry, no data collection, no accounts, no cloud sync. Everything stays with you — always.

Built for privacy-conscious users, developers, and anyone who wants to understand and improve their Android privacy — no technical knowledge required.

Open source. Available on F-Droid. No Google Play Store — intentionally.

Privamatic evaluates your device across security settings and surveillance tracking. Each check contributes to your overall privacy score out of 100 — starting at 100, with points deducted for detected issues, down to a minimum of 0.
These apps process conversations on cloud servers. They are detected and flagged for awareness but do not affect your privacy score.
Replay the welcome walkthrough on next app start
Always show the Advertising ID check regardless of the 180-day timer
Complete the Ad ID check first

Your Advertising ID is a unique identifier assigned to your device. Advertisers use it to track your activity across different apps and services, building a detailed profile of your interests and habits to serve targeted ads.
Deleting your Advertising ID removes it entirely. Android will no longer provide an advertising identifier to apps, breaking cross-app tracking.
What does deleting your Advertising ID do?
Replaces it with a new random ID
Disables all ads on your device
Removes the ID so advertisers can\'t link your activity across apps
Stops apps from tracking you permanently
Select the correct answer to unlock the next step
Understand exactly how private your Android is — and get a clear path to making it better.
45+ checks across security, tracking, and your apps. Weighted by real-world impact.
Privacy that fits real life. No paranoia, no impossible standards — just meaningful improvements.
